title: 2026/03/23 - JU [Part 1]
description: Monday, 23 March 2026 | Justice Committee Arms Bill Hearing of evidence: Mike McCormick Connor McCormick Matthew Fisken Jonathan Boston Corey Baker Graeme Easte Craig O'Brien Warwick Gibbs Alexander Gillespie Clementine Annabell Tim Clark David Wood Ross Mason Jim Smith Rene de Jongh Dennis Lally Paul Clark Don Hammond William Goldberg Southern Lakes Branch of New Zealand Deerstalkers Association Dave Rider Grant Fletcher Jeff Bryant Waitakere Smallbore Rifle Club John Dyer Andrew Lamb Gerard McMullen Jim Henry Tony Bruce Todd Barnes Colin Anderson Andrew Napier Glenn Soroka Gary Elmes

chunks.text: Come to the table. Yeah. Exactly, Stace. Okay. They didn't get our reporter. They didn't. Long time after. Camilla, and you'll get Dan Rosewall. Okay, the new man. Yeah. Okay, coming back. Right. Mr. McCormick. Yes, Mr. Chairman. We've got two Mr. McCormick's. Um, so we've got five minutes. Just, while people are sitting here, very helpful, these are su- short submissions.

Every member of the select committee has read these sub- your submission diligently. That's right. So we don't need to have, someone read it to us because you're just wasting your time, and I don't mean that in a derogatory way. I mean it in a way that often members will wanna ask questions, and the more that you spend talking or reading, it's less time for questions.

So what I'd suggest to you, and I'm talking to the room actually and anyone online, just summarize the very c- couple of the most important points, and then we can do... ask some questions. All right? Right. The time is yours. Yeah. Good morning. Thank you, Andrew. My name is Mike McCormick. I've been using firearms for a good forty-plus years, and I think that if more people were trained in the use of firearms, it would actually be a public good that would enhance, public safety.

It's a great challenging discipline, both mentally and physically, and, I think it's character building. Now, Section 4A of this act talks about, firearms ownership being a privilege. I think that's a direct contradiction of the Treaty of Waitangi. We have-- Even if Parliament shied away last year from establishing the principles of the Treaty of Waitangi, we're now left with the direct wording.

You have in the treaty, you have, undisturbed possession of forestry, fisheries and estates. Estates is firearms, of course. Um, but if you take the Māori, translation of taonga, that principle of it being a treasure. So there's no greater treasure than the possession and use of state-of-the-art self-preservation technology as being the most basic, fundamental human right.

Um, so from that, I get to the point where, I think the bill is incredibly long and wordy, and it's some four hundred sections or something. I would reduce it down to twenty-six words of any New Zealand citizen over the age of eighteen who is not subject to a firearms prohibition order may possess and use any arms and ammunition. Now, what we've done there is we've flipped the script.

So instead of focusing on a million odd people, when you run a regulatory regime like we do now, you've got two hundred and fifty thousand licensed firearms owners, plus their family members, plus their children, plus their mates who use their guns. You're targeting a million people. Whereas if we focused on firearms prohibition orders, you'd be targeting people on the national gang list and then people who've been convicted of violent crimes.

So maybe you're talking twenty or thirty thousand people, then you'd be far more effective, because I want effective legislation that's actually gonna deliver better public safety outcomes. So, then we get down to by focusing on the firearms prohibition orders also too, you're giving people their day in court, you're giving them the presumption of innocence and these, these are fundamental to, to our civil society. And you'd have a ten times the enforcement factor, right?

Because you're focusing on a lot smaller group of people who have actually caused harm to others. Um, so then we get on to also there's a-- the purpose of the bill is to promote the use of firearms, and I endorse this. It's a great idea, and I would urge the committee to recommend a budget be set aside for establishing ranges up and down the country to actually, with the equipment for using those ranges to develop a much healthier attitude to firearms, to demystify it and to educate people. So, I think that covers.

You've all read my submission. Do you have any questions for me? Right. Um, Mr. Bates. Thanks, Chair. So just so I'm clear, Mike, the intention of your rewrite recommendation is the entire bill getting rewritten to that? Yes. You could reduce it to twenty-six words. Okay.

Yes. So my supplementary question to that is, so I get the point you're trying to make and appreciate, the, ability people should have to be able to use firearms in New Zealand for legitimate purposes. I suppose the challenge I'd have with doing that is that then you, you'd have to issue a whole lot of prohibition orders to people who clearly wouldn't be, appropriate to have access to a firearm at the moment.

Now, whether that be for the examples you've used, like gang membership, et cetera, whether that be for, mental health, challenges or concerns, and I, I wonder if that, would at all be practical. No, the burden of proof is on the state. Before you deny me of my, my, my, my fundamental human rights, you, you need to provide evidence that I'm causing harm.

So if you have to issue a whole lot of firearms prohibition orders, the police have already got the national gang list. So I take, I take the point you're trying to make, but we don't give you the right to drive a car and then work out whether or not you've got the ability to have a license. We, we do take people through a process before they have access to driving a car. Yeah, right. So yeah, that's what I'm saying.

So if we funded ranges up and down the country, we'd, we'd educate a whole lot of people. I agree. I accept your submission. That's a great idea. Yeah. All right. Hey, thank you. Um, for your submission this morning. Oh, okay. Your five minutes is up. Thank you, Andrew. I'd love to chat longer. Thank you. Now we've got another, Mr. Mc- McCormick.

Good morning. Uh, good morning. Thank you all for sitting here to hear my submission. My name is, Connor. I've been actively shooting and participating in sport shooting and hunting and collecting for about ten years, and quite actively involved. I think to summarize the three main points of my submission, which I thank you all for taking the time to read, is that the Arms Bill is very long and very complicated, and the three main focuses should be: we should remove the register entirely, we should, reinstate the possession

and use of semi-automatic firearms for anyone that fits the definition of fit and proper person, and we should ensure that the bill-Uh, completely separates the police from any administration of the day-to-day operation of the Arms Act. Uh, so when I say that, I don't mean that they should have no involvement. The bill outlines quite well the chief executive should be separate, and that they work closely with the police.

Um, there's a subsection there, I believe it's Clause 300, which covers that the police, uh... well, that the, Arms- the new Arms Act administration would be an agency of the police. I don't believe this promotes, the genuine independence. I think that the police do an excellent job of enforcing the law and pr- prosecuting people generally when they break them, and that the administration of the Act to encourage compliance and, improvements for public safety should be worked through an administration point of view and they should

have a close working relationship at the top, but not day-to-day examples. I'll give you a case for why the registry should be removed from this case. Um, one is that the registry puts a huge amount of cost of resources and time, both of people and money, on tracing serial numbers from inani- inanimate objects where I think the previous Arms Act had it right. Um, that Arms Act was also ninety-six clauses, not the four hundred we have today, where it focused much more heavily on the person that uses firearms and not, tracking particular firearms.

And you've got a ten-round mag and you've got a semi-auto, those are used very safely and appropriately for many years, before we had the, before... yeah, meant they were used very safely and effectively for many years, and that the owner should always be on the state to prove that somebody is not fit and proper, and not placing the burden of proof on, innocent people. Um, and you also have to consider that with every level of complexity, there is more, issue with maintaining consistency and accuracy with any register.

Uh, put aside the cost element, which I think on its own is enough, along with the, the, the burden of proof. Um, I had a case where I submitted some paperwork to the police for putting pistol items on my license, and the permit number had three items on it. The police told me that they wouldn't, do it, that I'd have to enter it myself. And when I went home, they'd put four items from a three-item permit, on the register. It took quite a bit of talking and convincing to say, "Actually, the fourth item you put on there doesn't exist.

I have the permit number. That was a human error." If you compound that over the course of years, months, that that register may be in place along with the cost, you would lose any form of accuracy and any, any minor benefit that you may, on a very excluded and isolated incident, prove that it has benefit would be lost due to inaccuracy alone. Um, I just think, yeah, that those would be my three, major points.

Remove the register, bring back the semi-autos, and, make sure the police are completely separate from the administration. Uh, thank you. So can I just, um... You're saying that they had four items, and it took... it was a lot of difficulty getting removed, removing the fourth. Y- yes. What... Why was that? You know, there's obviously an obvious, obvious answer, but- Yes... were they wanting to, for you to prove there wasn't a fourth?

Uh, what was sort of the process? Well, it's, it's, I guess, the process was that, and you have to, because of how the register was rushed in its implementation and the scope of it, it required everyone to call the register to, to deal with them on a day-to-day basis to add items to your name. The IT infrastructure doesn't exist. Um, even if it did, it probably wouldn't be secure.

Um, so you had to call and they put the keystrokes in themselves. Um, and so when I called them up, they said, "This is the permit," and they outlined what the... They said there were four items. There was only three. There was a conversion kit. There was, and two pistols. The conversion kit is classed as a firearm, but they believed that that conversion kit also had other parts which made it the fourth- Right... fully functioning item.

Right. And you're trying to explain this over the phone. Right. Mr. Stevens. Oh, but, and just, just staying on this, and then they gave you contradictory advice. Is that what you're saying? Yes. So when I went- Yes. You had to enter it, but then they entered it. They said, they said, "Yes, you can take... Here, here's your permit. Um, here's the permit number. This is the permit to possess. Uh, take it in and to the register and put it in." So I, when I went to do that, I noticed that the items that I was trying to put in were already there.

And they said, "Oh, no, we do that for you." I said, "Well, I was told that I, I didn't have to do it- Right... that it's my obligation," which caused the rather long, conversation on the phone to try and prove that something doesn't exist. Yeah. Which is rather difficult. It, it was eventually solved, but the, the problem is that- That was out of trouble. Yeah. All right. Thank you. Thank you for your submission.
Okay. Thank you for your time. Now, we've got Mr. Fisken. Here we are. Good. Good morning. Good morning.

All right, when you're ready. Yeah, yeah. Mr.- Oh. The floor is yours. Brilliant. Well, good morning, select committee, and thank you all for your time this morning to hear my submission. I'd just like to firstly thank the Honorable Minister Nicole McKee and her research staff for the time and dedication that it took to bring this bill to the House. And I'd also like to commend this government for recognizing the need to repeal and replace the Arms Act 1983.

While I'm supportive of the intent of this bill, I firmly believe that there needs to be a number of amendments made to ensure that this legislation will be fit for purpose, protecting both public safety and the rights of law-abiding licensed firearm owners. For my first point, I strongly support reintroducing lawful ownership of centerfire semi-automatic firearms with detachable magazines, such as AR-15s and other modern sporting rifle platforms, for legitimate sporting purposes.

And I'd recommend that this be done by creating an endorsement system similar to the current B endorsement that we have for pistols. Uh, I also recommend that limited capacity semi-automatic centerfire rifles with non-detachable magazines, for example, the Norinco SKS or M1 Grand, be available on a standard A category license. Um, now I appreciate that most of you may not be familiar with those firearm types. I've actually got a reference document which I can provide to the committee staff afterwards.

Um, additionally, I believe this should be expanded to include modern sporting rifles which have been permanently modified or manufactured to have a fixed ten-round magazine. Now, this will actually help to increase public safety by assisting with re-legitimizing what are currently gray market firearms, and those are prohibited firearms that are unaccounted for following the twenty nineteen amnesty and buyback scheme. Additionally, this will also help with restoring some of the rights of licensed firearm owners that were unjustifiably

taken through a knee-jerk reaction by the last government.Moving on to the topic of the firearms registry, I ultimately very firmly believe that the existing registry of A category firearms must be abolished and its existing data be destroyed. And we can see from overseas examples such as Canada, which has a general, which had a general firearms registry from 1995 to 2012, that it had no effect on reducing crime, it was highly inaccurate, and ended up costing Canadian taxpayers more than a billion dollars.

Additionally, there are security and privacy concerns. In this country, we've seen a number of high-profile data leaks, most recently with Manage My Health, and I hold a very grave concern that when, not if, a matter of when a registry leak occurs, it will enable criminals to be able to identify and prioritize burglary victims, thus endangering licensed firearm owners, their families, and the community as a whole.

In closing, I support the direction of this bill, but I urge the committee to carefully consider my recommendations and ensure that the resulting legislation is effective, fair, and reasonable. Thank you. I'll now take some questions. Right. Wide-ranging submission. Now, first one I've got is Mr. Stevenson. Thanks, Matt. I'm just... One of the other things you, in your submission you support is the Firearms, Licensing Review Committee.

Yes. Why do you think that's an important body to establish? Well, currently we have a system where should, a licensed firearms owner, end up in a legal difficulty and potentially face revocation, the only option is essentially judicial review through the district courts. Um, now that is an extremely expensive, task, an expense which most licensed firearm owners simply can't front up to.

Yes. Not to mention, I, I know it can take more than a year to actually get a day in court for a judicial review as well. So when you consider the time and the money, it's, only really an avenue that's open to a very small percentage of the two hundred and thirty thousand licensed firearm owners. I, I think this review would make, justice more accessible and faster for everyone.

Okay. Can I ask a question, um- Yes... because you're a relatively young person. Yes. Um, you're, you're advocating the, retaining the age of 16. That's correct. And you, you got your license at 16, I see. That's right, mm-hmm. Um, just take us through the process of, of getting that license. What did you have to go through? Did you, like, did you get arranged?

Did you- Yeah. So at the time I was already, a member of the Invercargill Pistol Club, and I had been for a number of years. Um, I had to do a application, which was a paper version at the time. Things have changed significantly since then. Um, I needed to have two referees, one being a next of kin, one being someone who knows me well that is older than 20. For that, I believe we used the pistol club president.

Um, I needed to have an in-person interview done at my home, where they also inspected my storage facilities. Um, I've no doubt they would've also done, a background check and criminal record check, as well as to look for any other information that may or may not have been held on me at the time. Um, all up, the process of, if I recall correctly, took about four months.

Um, but like I'm, I'm remembering back to 2019, so that's- Yeah. Yeah. Cool. All right. Hey, thank you. Thanks. Um, that's a good submission. Uh, thank you very much. All right. Thanks very much. Now, members, the next person is caught in traffic, so I'm hoping that, Jonathan Boston's here. I'm here.

Yeah. It's Professor, isn't it? Well, if you wish. Emeritus, emeritus now. Well, yeah, well, that makes it even more grander. Well, thank you very much for the opportunity to make an oral submission, to this committee. Uh, I, I will take it as read that you have read my, my submission, so I'll just make two quick points.

Uh, in my view, the organizational form, that is proposed for this new arms regulator is, neither desirable nor justified, a- and I've recommended, an alternative structure. Uh, and secondly, I have serious concerns of a constitutional nature and a practical nature, about the risks associated with the minister responsible making, in effect, the appointment of the, chief executive of the arms, regulator.

Um, so, I've set out in, in my submission why I have those concerns. Uh, and the basic point is that if the aim of this, process is to enhance, public trust and confidence, then I think the institutional arrangements and the appointment process will have precisely the opposite consequence and will place the minister, in a potentially difficult, embarrassing position.

Right. Good. And first question is from, Ms. Vanusi Walters. Thank you, Mr. Chair, and thank you, Professor Boston. Um, I wanted to ask a question about something you didn't mention, but perhaps you have an opinion on, in terms of the minister's powers. So the minister also has significant discretionary powers in terms of the interpretation of what I would consider key, sections of the bill.

Right. Um, in your view, are those an appropriate use of secondary powers? So forgive me, but I haven't studied all aspects of the bills. Uh, m- my principal concern was with the organizational arrangements and the minister's, involvement in the appointment of the arms regulator. Um, as a, as a general comment, I, I would have reservations about ministers having significant discretionary powers in, in this kind of field.

I mean, one of the reasons for delegating responsibilities to, either independent or quasi-independent entities, is to ensure that, key decisions are, are made, by people who are deemed to have relevant expertise and, and distance from the government of the day.

So it's not that we shouldn't have discretionary powers in some circumstances but-Um, I think they should be limited in the interest actually of the minister as well. Because if the minister's exercising discretionary powers, then of course the minister can be under enormous pressure a- and, and can be exposed to, a variety of risks.

Okay, we've got a couple more questions. First one, Todd. Thanks. Second one, Todd Simpson. Thanks for being here. Mm. Just so I'm clear, so I'm just with that re- in relation to the arms regulator- Yeah... so you, I think you're saying if you, if the goal of, of true independence from the police is what's wanted, then there are different ways of doing it, like a Crown entity, like the independent...

Is this basically what you're saying, there could be better ways to make sure that- Yes... it's actually truly independent? If you wanted full- Yeah, which is the stated, stated aim of the bill. Yeah... independence. But I, I'm suspicious about the extent to which you could realistically have complete independence in a context where the police are going to be holding a lot of the information that the arms regulator will need.

So, you know, the current model at the moment i- in which, in which the entity is e- essentially a business unit within the police- Yeah... is in fact, as I understand it, the model which is normal internationally, and there are good reasons, there are good reasons for that. But if you wanted to have additional separation, then it seems to me something like a departmental agency would be, would be preferable. I appreciate that under the, Public Service Act, technically the police aren't a department- Mm... so technically you can't have a d- departmental agency, but I...

that, that could be dealt with- Mo- that's the kind of model that you're thinking of... that could be dealt with through technical changes to legislation. Yeah. Right. Mr. Kel Bates. Thanks, Chair. And flowing on from that, in your 1B recommendation, other than that enabling of a public service commissioner to be the appointer of the chief executive as opposed directly the minister- Mm... what other practical implementat- practical changes would 1B, create?

Well, I mean, the critical issue here is who should appoint the arms regulator, and I'm making the case that, it would be preferable to have, the arms regulator appointed in a normal- Yes... process of the kind that we use for appointing departmental chief executives or the heads of departmental agencies.

Uh, the model that's proposed in this legislation is extremely unusual, and I think constitutionally risky. So, so I appreciate that. So I'm asking- Yes... other than changing essentially who appoints the chief executive- Yes... w- would there be any other practical implementation, changes if your recommendation- Yes. Well, I mean-... 1B was adopted... I mean, among the changes that would be inevitable if you did that would be that the minister would not have a role in, determining or influencing the terms and conditions of appointment.

Yeah. Anything else in there? Well, there may be some other things. I'm sorry. I, I'd have to- No, no, it's fine. No, no... think that through carefully. Sure. Yeah, yeah. All right. Hey, thank you very much, Charles. All right. Of course you've been here before, but thank you very much for your- Yes. My pleasure... input. And I wish you well. All right. Thank you. Thank you. And now, are you Mr. Corey Baker? Yes. Can, apologize for being- No, no, no.

Uh, we... No. Fine. All right. Thank you. Thanks, Professor. Thank you. I'm just glad you made it. Yeah, me too. Chairman, is the volume on your earpiece, can you turn the volume up or is it just fixed? Can we turn this up a bit? Yeah. Yeah, we're gonna tr- we'll have a look. Okay, cool. All right. Good morning. Good morning.

Um, we've all read your submission, so just- Oh, I would-... summar- summarize your key points and then- Yeah, so, the submission was, I had problems actually submitting my larger file, so it's not representative of- Okay. Well, y- you talk- I had technical issues- Yeah... when it was originally, so it will be short and odd. Um, first speaker had the same concerns as myself.

It's about the, the executive powers and how it's separated from the minister to, it's essentially the processes of how the appointment of, the authority is taking place. Um, like first speaker said, it's, it is quite a, it's a,

constitutionally it is a bit of a stretch because it's, it's, failing due process. Um, this will probably be repeated through the day, so I know I've only got my five-minute allocation, so I want to keep it brief. That's why you, you talk about what you think is important. Yeah. Uh, I, I, I think that the authority should s- have some branch of, public representation through like a, through like a democratic election, within the body of the firearms holders.
Um, I think it's a really crucial aspect of,

it, it's a, it's a crucial, aspect of having a holistic approach to the, the, the firearms regulation as a whole. Um, I think it, the net benefit for education, socialization, making sure that, firearms license holders are so- it sounds odd, but socializing, and it's a really good stepping stone of getting, people in together.

It's also, it also addresses kind of, mental health risk and factors and, you know, it, it, it brings people from rural communities into a kind of, not a union, but essentially a, a body that's representative. And I think it might be quite handy for the regulator as well to have that kind of voice representative in the processes.
Um, one thing I wanted to briefly just, make mention is that there is a,

s- Section 69, dash 1, it- it's talking about the, a proper person to hold firearms. The language around it's really kind of iffy. Um-And it also leaves a lot of discretion to the executive, which I think that needs to be a bit more robust in its, in its legal wording.

Um, the, the one aspect that I wanted to address with it though is that there's a, a conflict between terms. So, it makes reference to protection order, including temporary protection orders. And I just needed to make note that in, section one it says that, I think it's dash e, wherever I've got it here.

Yeah, the, the section... I think it's section 60... Is it, 69-1 and, 68 clash with each other. So one, one makes reference to the prote- temporary protection order being outside of the scope of refusal of the application of a license. And then the second make men- makes mention that the temporary protection orders are inc- included inside the, the scope of refusal.

Um, the only reason that I have issue with this is-... one firsthand experience with it. Um, but it's... I understand from safety protocol and safety measures that it, it needs to be, presented on the forefront. But the, the judicial process needs to be completed and we...

The threshold of having proof and also innocent before proven guilty is like a cornerstone of our principle, and the protection order falls underneath that threshold. The temporary protection order, a TPO, falls well within, well under that threshold of... So I, I think just as a, um...
To address that, it just needs to be, kind of revised or removed from the, from the list of,

yeah, the, the, the list of, legal denials for a licensee holder. Um, th- on that as well, just with the, the gang affiliate and the... Because that is the one of the most dramatic shifts. So I've got no affiliation, I don't, I oppose gangs having firearms and...

But it becomes a fun- a constitutional kind of breach of the bill of rights, I, I believe, and it's also a watering down of a, a fundamental pi- pillar that we have and a protection for the public. So, that's something that I'm, yeah, I'm, I'm kind of torn between it. I think it needs a little bit more of a look, only because of the executive powers gives a subject of purview to, to the threshold of who can and who can't.

The, the, the good thing with the new proposals is that, is that there's a really robust, legal strengthening of our, of consequences of breaches of, of firearm misappropriation. And I just feel that that's a strong enough legal, co- that's a strong enough legal kind of, fence around anything that needs to be protected.

If, if a, if a gang member has any of the, listed breaches that puts them outside of the scope of being able to apply for, the firearms license itself, I think that's a strong enough threshold. If they're law-abiding and they don't have anything against their, any, any ch- any prior existing charges, I think it might be a, yeah, I, I think it would be a constitutional issue.

Right. Hey, thank you. Um, we're a bit over time. Yes. Can I just ask-... can I just ask on your very first point. Mm-hmm. Are... That representation of firearms owners or holders- Mm-hmm... are you suggesting that they should have a right to be on, sitting on the agency's board- I-... or just as a reference group?

I think it's really important that either they have a right to be, part of it or part of the process of how the executive is appointed. So I noticed that one of the prerequisites is, that they... it's, it's lawyers that are involved in it, so, those that are, sworn into the bar.

Right. And I, I think it can become quite litigious as opposed to- Right... it's losing its pract- practicality, and I think you need that kind of, boots on the ground representation- Yeah... and, and kind of committee. It's, it's really valuable. Like- Sure... hunters have, invaluable experience, farmers have an- Yeah... insight that's second to none. Um, but yeah.

All right. Hey, thank you. Very good submission. It was a bit different from what you wrote, but- Yeah, yeah. It's good. Yeah... but, but very good. I- Thank you... I just compliment you on your submi- your, talk today. Thank you. Yeah. No worries. Thank you. Right. Now we're gonna go to, Mr. East, is it? On, on Zoom. On Zoom. Are you there?

Good morning. Good morning. How you going? We can't see you, but, that's... Oh, there you are. Good. Hey, we've got five minutes with you. We've read your submission. Um, looking forward to hearing what you've got to say. Uh, good morning. Um, I began my submission with reference to two important clauses right at near the beginning of the bill, which I'd like to seeWell, they are in the draft.

I hope they survive the process. But I would like the committee to bear these in mind while they are listening, today. And but in particular, Clause 4A, that the possession and use of arms is a privilege. This is the exact opposite of the often quoted right to bear arms in the American Constitution.

There is no right to bear arms. The, the preamble to that particular part, amendment begins by saying, "For the formation of a well-formed mili- militia," or words to that effect. Um, that context is usually completely forgotten. Anyway, I think I've made that point.

I'll skip over the other points and move to the last point I made, which is that the Arms Advisory Group, in the legislat- or, or in the draft legislation, it comprises a balance of people from both the firearms owning and non-firearm owning community, including people who are concerned from a health perspective about the mitigation of harms from firearms.

So contrary to some of the earlier speakers who seem to think that there is some God-given right to bear arms, and also that, they have the expertise, that's to say firearms owners, to be on this, Arms Advisory Group. Um, I grant them that they have that expertise, but they also have a particular interest, which is not necessarily the public interest.

So we need to make sure... And part of the problem is that the minister gets to appoint that group. Um, so I certainly hope the select committee will be keeping a watching brief on the minister as to how she uses that authority or power, and call her to account if she's not, achieving the balance that's spoken of.

Um, anyway, I'll stop there if there's any questions. Right. Well, first one I've got from is Vanusi Walters. Um, thank you very much for your submission, Graham. Um, on your point three in your submission, you talk about reducing the licensing period and having probationary licenses, for first time applicants. Is that on the basis of another jurisdiction, or what's the basis of your proposal there?

Straight out common sense, the idea of a probationary period. We do it with, motor vehicle holders. They begin as probationers, and if they prove to be, safe and effective drivers, they then move on to the longer-term license. Um, so that seems to me to be a very common sense approach.

If you have someone who's, fr- at least from an official point of view, never had anything to do with firearms, suddenly apply. That is actually quite a big step. Um, and I, I accept that it shouldn't be, be, be, you know, there shouldn't be too many bureaucratic hurdles, et cetera.

But the, nevertheless, there should be, you know, the vetting process, et cetera, to make sure that they are, in the minister's words, fit and proper. Um, so I, I think that two-step process, rather than have an incredibly short, re-licensing period, that it be just that first license, be a temporary or probationary.
Okay. All right. Hey, thank you very much for your submission this morning. Thank you. Right. Now, we're gonna go to Mr. Craig O'Brien, who's on Zoom as well. Craig, are you with us this morning?

Good day. Hey, Craig. Are you there? Yes, I am. It's just unmuting. Oh, yeah. That's cool. Hey, we've got five minutes with you. Okay. Thank you very much for the opportunity to t- talk to you this morning. Um, my background is IT network engineer for the government, and part of my role is looking after classified computer networks.
